claim: 95% of all generative AI pilots are failing according to MIT reports
actionable: Use this statistic to justify investment in proper evaluation systems and iterative improvement processes rather than hoping AI projects succeed without systematic testing
source_title: How to Systematically Setup LLM Evals (Metrics, Unit Tests, LLM-as-a-Judge)

claim: Three core challenges in LLM development: understanding data at scale, specification gaps between desired and implemented behavior, and inconsistent/unpredictable model behavior across different inputs
actionable: When developing LLM applications, systematically address all three challenges—use Langfuse to monitor real data patterns, document expected outputs clearly, and test against diverse edge cases beyond happy path scenarios
source_title: How to Systematically Setup LLM Evals (Metrics, Unit Tests, LLM-as-a-Judge)

claim: The analyze-measure-improve cycle is essential for continuous LLM application improvement: collect failure examples, translate into quantitative metrics, then refine prompts and architecture
actionable: Implement a continuous feedback loop where you categorize real failures in production, create boolean or numerical metrics around them, and systematically test prompt/model changes against a test suite of these failures
source_title: How to Systematically Setup LLM Evals (Metrics, Unit Tests, LLM-as-a-Judge)

claim: LLM evaluation has three levels: unit tests (fast, automated), human/model evaluation (weekly/bi-weekly, requires human involvement), and A/B testing with real users (high cost, major releases only)
actionable: Start with level 1 unit tests using assertions on structured outputs, progress to level 2 by having humans label 100 examples and training an LLM-as-judge to match human judgments, only move to level 3 after you have confidence in levels 1-2
source_title: How to Systematically Setup LLM Evals (Metrics, Unit Tests, LLM-as-a-Judge)

claim: To create aligned LLM evaluators, collect predictions, generate LLM critiques, get human evaluations on the same data, compare judge vs human judgments, iterate on evaluator prompt until sufficient agreement
actionable: Create an Excel sheet with 10-100 examples of input-output pairs; have a human expert label each; have an LLM judge label each using your evaluator prompt; calculate agreement percentage; use meta-prompting on top LLM to optimize the evaluator prompt based on discrepancies
source_title: How to Systematically Setup LLM Evals (Metrics, Unit Tests, LLM-as-a-Judge)

claim: Meta-prompting—asking a powerful LLM to optimize your evaluator prompt by analyzing discrepancies between human and model judgments—significantly improves LLM judge alignment with human experts
actionable: After running a batch of 100 evaluations where human and LLM judge disagree, export the data and prompt Claude/GPT-4 with: 'Here are examples where evaluations differ. Optimize this prompt so disagreements drop to near 0%' and integrate the improved prompt
source_title: How to Systematically Setup LLM Evals (Metrics, Unit Tests, LLM-as-a-Judge)

claim: Common evaluation mistakes: tool-first thinking (buying a platform instead of understanding your problem), obsessing over generic metrics (4.2 helpfulness scores), avoiding real data inspection, and using unaligned LLM judges without validation
actionable: Before buying any evaluation tool or using an off-shelf metric, manually inspect 50+ real data points, start with binary good/bad judgments, and always validate any LLM judge against human labels first
source_title: How to Systematically Setup LLM Evals (Metrics, Unit Tests, LLM-as-a-Judge)

claim: A principal engineer at Zapier spends 3 hours per day examining traces and logs to understand real data flowing through their system—this is critical, not optional
actionable: Block off 2-3 hours weekly to systematically review real user traces in Langfuse or your logging platform; don't rely solely on metrics dashboards; look at actual requests and failures
source_title: How to Systematically Setup LLM Evals (Metrics, Unit Tests, LLM-as-a-Judge)

claim: Success indicators for evaluation-driven workflows: you can deploy changes confidently, failures are caught before users see them, system behavior is well understood, improvements compound over time
actionable: Track these four signals for your LLM application: deployment confidence (can you roll out changes?), pre-user error detection rate, team understanding of system behavior, and trend in improvement rate—if any are degrading, invest in evaluation infrastructure
source_title: How to Systematically Setup LLM Evals (Metrics, Unit Tests, LLM-as-a-Judge)

claim: Unit test assertions should check: structured output fields are valid types and within expected ranges, category values are in allowed list, and for specific inputs expected outputs are correct
actionable: For each LLM-generated structured output, write Python assertions checking field types (float, str), value ranges (confidence 0-1), and set membership (category in ['billing','tech','general']); store 20+ test examples in JSON and run after every system prompt change
source_title: How to Systematically Setup LLM Evals (Metrics, Unit Tests, LLM-as-a-Judge)

claim: Reference-based metrics work when you know the correct answer (exact match, semantic similarity, SQL correctness); reference-free metrics apply when multiple correct outputs exist (tone, no hallucination, format compliance, safety)
actionable: For structured tasks with one right answer, use reference-based metrics; for open-ended tasks like customer support, build reference-free metrics and use LLM judges to evaluate tone, helpfulness, and safety
source_title: How to Systematically Setup LLM Evals (Metrics, Unit Tests, LLM-as-a-Judge)

claim: Never skip the human evaluation step when building LLM judges—always collect 100+ human-labeled examples first, then create an LLM evaluator aligned to those humans, then track human-model agreement over time
actionable: Before deploying any automated LLM evaluation, create a gold standard by having domain experts label 100 test cases; measure agreement with your LLM judge; only deploy if agreement > 80%; continue sampling and re-measuring monthly for evaluation drift
source_title: How to Systematically Setup LLM Evals (Metrics, Unit Tests, LLM-as-a-Judge)
